Policy Name/Number: SCR184 – Requesting the Hawaii Civil Rights Commission to Examine Algorithmic Decision Systems
Summary: Requests the Hawaii Civil Rights Commission to examine how existing anti-discrimination laws apply to algorithmic and automated decision systems and to assess risks of bias and disparate impact.
Healthcare Implications: Signals increased regulatory scrutiny of algorithmic bias in healthcare settings, including clinical decision support, risk stratification tools, and access-related algorithms; may inform future enforcement or legislation addressing equity in health AI systems.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HB191 – Medical Professionals, Titles, and Nonhuman Entities
Summary: Amends Delaware law governing professional titles to clarify that nonhuman entities, including artificial intelligence systems, may not use titles or representations implying licensure or professional status reserved for human practitioners.
Healthcare Implications: Directly restricts AI systems, including clinical chatbots and digital health assistants, from presenting as licensed professionals; reinforces disclosure and scope-of-practice boundaries, reducing risk of patient confusion and strengthening accountability in AI-enabled care delivery.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: AL SB63 – Health Care Plans; To Regulate The Use of Artificial Intelligence in Determinations of Coverage
Summary: Regulates the use of artificial intelligence by insurers in making determinations of coverage under health benefit plans, including requirements related to clinical review, disclosures, and annual certification.
Healthcare Implications: Directly constrains payer use of artificial intelligence in utilization review and coverage determinations. Health plans must maintain clinical oversight and documentation, while providers and patients gain added protection against algorithm-only decisions.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: LB525 – Adopt the Agricultural Data Privacy Act and the Conversational Artificial Intelligence Safety Act
Summary: Establishes the Conversational Artificial Intelligence Safety Act, requiring operators of AI systems that simulate human interaction to provide clear disclosure, implement safeguards against harmful or deceptive interactions, and meet transparency and accountability requirements.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to patient-facing conversational AI tools, including mental health chatbots and symptom checkers, by requiring disclosure that users are interacting with AI and imposing safety guardrails; increases compliance obligations for digital health developers and reduces risk of patient deception or overreliance on automated systems.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: ME LD2082 – An Act to Regulate the Use of Artificial Intelligence in Providing Certain Mental Health Services
Summary: Restricts the use of artificial intelligence in therapy and psychotherapy services by allowing only supervised administrative or supplementary support, with notice, consent, confidentiality, and professional responsibility requirements.
Healthcare Implications: Creates direct guardrails for AI in mental health care by prohibiting AI from making independent therapeutic decisions or directly communicating with clients. Behavioral health practices, providers, and vendors will need to align workflows, disclosures, and data handling with clinical and privacy standards.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: TN HB1470 – Qualified Mental Health Professional AI Misrepresentation
Summary: Prohibits a person from developing or deploying an artificial intelligence system that advertises or represents to the public that it is, or can act as, a qualified mental health professional, and subjects violations to consumer-protection enforcement.
Healthcare Implications: Bars misleading AI therapy claims and reduces the risk that patients, caregivers, or providers treat a chatbot as a licensed mental health professional. Health systems and vendors should review marketing, disclaimers, and QA processes for mental-health tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: OR SB1546 – Artificial Intelligence Companions
Summary: Requires operators of artificial intelligence companions and companion platforms to disclose when users are interacting with artificial output, adopt protocols for suicidal ideation or self-harm, and make certain disclosures when a user is believed to be a minor. Also requires annual public reporting of referrals to crisis resources.
Healthcare Implications: Creates direct guardrails for consumer-facing companion bots that may be used for emotional support, mental health, or wellness. Developers and health-facing platforms should add disclosures, crisis workflows, and youth protections to reduce self-harm risk.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: ID S1297 – Conversational AI Safety Act
Summary: Establishes the Conversational AI Safety Act by requiring operators of AI companions and companion platforms to disclose when users are interacting with artificial output, adopt suicide- and self-harm-prevention protocols, and add protections when a user appears to be a minor.
Healthcare Implications: Creates direct safety and disclosure duties for consumer AI systems that may be used for emotional support or mental-health-adjacent conversations. Health systems and vendors should treat it as a baseline compliance issue for chatbots, patient support tools, and crisis-routing workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: NY S8828 – AI Model Developer Transparency Update
Summary: Amends New York’s frontier-model transparency law to expand developer transparency and reporting requirements and support a state oversight office for model governance and disclosures.
Healthcare Implications: Indirectly affects clinical and biomedical AI by increasing transparency and reporting expectations for frontier-model developers whose systems may be embedded in health tools. Providers and vendors using large models should treat the law as part of upstream governance and due-diligence review.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: WA HB2225 – AI Companion Chatbots
Summary: Regulates artificial intelligence companion chatbots by requiring user notice that the user is interacting with artificial output, protections for minors, and protocols for detecting and responding to suicidal ideation or self-harm.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to consumer-facing companion bots that may be used for emotional support or mental-health-adjacent interactions. Developers and health-facing platforms should add disclosure, escalation, and minor-protection workflows to reduce risk.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: WA HB1170 – AI Content Disclosure and Provenance
Summary: Requires providers of certain generative AI systems to make an AI-detection tool available to users, offer users the option of including disclosures in content created or altered by the systems, and include latent disclosures in generated content.
Healthcare Implications: Relevant to patient-facing health communications, clinical content workflows, and consumer health tools that rely on generative content. Health systems and vendors should account for provenance labeling and disclosure in digital communications and content moderation.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB5395 – Prior Authorization Determination Process
Summary: Strengthens Washington’s prior-authorization framework for private health insurance by adding transparency, reporting, and patient-protection requirements, including guardrails on the use of artificial intelligence in medical-necessity determinations and prohibitions on AI-only denials, delays, or modifications of care.
Healthcare Implications: Directly constrains payer and utilization-management use of AI in coverage decisions by requiring human clinical review for denials based on medical necessity, auditability of AI policies, periodic performance review, and reporting on AI-aided denials. This is a high-salience health-AI governance law for payers, providers, and patients.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Privacy & Data
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB0319 – Health Insurance Preauthorization Amendments
Summary: Updates Utah’s health-insurance preauthorization law by adding definitions for artificial intelligence and generative artificial intelligence, tightening decision timelines, expanding reporting, requiring clearer adverse-determination notices, and reinforcing clinician-led review of medical-necessity denials.
Healthcare Implications: Directly affects how insurers use AI in prior authorization and utilization management by requiring disclosure and reporting around AI-linked workflows while preserving human clinical judgment in adverse determinations. The law is highly relevant to payer oversight, provider burden, and patient access to timely medically necessary care.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: IN HB1271 – Payment of Health Claims
Summary: Prohibits certain downcoding practices in health insurance claims and restricts the use of automated processes, systems, or tools, including artificial intelligence, in claim submission and adjudication without appropriate review.
Healthcare Implications: Would directly impact payer and provider use of AI in claims processing by limiting automated downcoding and requiring human oversight in utilization management workflows. Health plans and providers will need to adjust claims review, documentation, and audit processes to ensure compliance.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: S06953 – Responsible AI Safety and Education (RAISE) Act
Summary: Requires “large developers” of high-compute frontier AI models to create and publish safety plans, run risk assessments, and report significant safety incidents to the state; authorizes a new oversight office to monitor frontier developers and issue annual reports on AI safety.
Healthcare Implications: Indirect but meaningful for health AI: sets baseline safety and incident-reporting expectations for large frontier models that underlie many clinical, biomedical, and health-adjacent tools, influencing vendors’ safety practices and the risk profile of models used in care delivery, research, and population health.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: S07599 – Automated Decision-Making in Government Agencies
Summary: Amends the state technology, education, and civil service laws to regulate automated decision-making systems used by government agencies; requires “meaningful human review” for high-stakes uses, mandates periodic impact assessments (including testing for bias, privacy, cybersecurity, and public-health/safety risks), and requires disclosure of existing ADM tools to the governor and legislature.
Healthcare Implications: Directly affects state health, Medicaid, mental-health, and social-service programs that use AI/ADM for eligibility, fraud detection, case prioritization, or enforcement. Agencies must evaluate public-health/safety risks, bias, and privacy impacts before deploying ADM systems, and keep humans in the loop for decisions affecting benefits, rights, and welfare, reducing the risk of unsafe or discriminatory health-related decisions.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias; Privacy & Data
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB243 – Companion Chatbots
Summary: Requires operators of companion‑chatbot platforms to clearly disclose AI, implement protocols to prevent suicidal ideation/self‑harm content, and publish details of those protocols; establishes annual reporting to the Office of Suicide Prevention starting 2027.
Healthcare Implications: Behavioral‑health and wellness chatbots used by providers/plans must add conspicuous AI disclosures, suicide‑prevention safeguards, and reporting. Providers and plans should adjust oversight, auditing, and incident‑response workflows to align with the statute.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: AB489 – Health Care Professions: Deceptive Terms or Letters: Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Clarifies that laws barring use of protected health‑professional titles/letters to imply licensure apply to entities that develop or deploy AI; prohibits such uses in AI advertising or functionality and subjects violators to board oversight/enforcement.
Healthcare Implications: Provider and payer chatbots/assistants must not suggest they are licensed clinicians (e.g., 'doctor', 'nurse'). Providers and payers should update UI/marketing, disclaimers, and QA to prevent implied licensure; add checks in procurement and model governance.
Keyword Tags: Transparency & Governance; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: AB979 – California Cybersecurity Integration Center: Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Directs the California Cybersecurity Integration Center, working with the Office of Information Security and the Government Operations Agency, to publish by January 1, 2027 a California AI Cybersecurity Collaboration Playbook coordinating threat and vulnerability information-sharing across cyber and AI communities. Requires state AI contractors to share relevant threats with a designated state entity and protects shared indicators from disclosure under the California Public Records Act.
Healthcare Implications: Indirect but relevant: Because the California Health and Human Services Agency participates in the California Cybersecurity Integration Center, health agencies and hospital vendors on state-linked systems should align incident reporting and business associate agreement terms with the playbook’s sharing and confidentiality rules to manage AI-related cyber risk.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB53 – Artificial Intelligence Models: Large Developers
Summary: Regulates frontier (highly advanced, large-scale, general-purpose) models and their large developers. Requires a published safety framework, transparency reports with catastrophic-risk summaries, 15-day critical-incident reporting to California's Governor's Office of Emergency Services, whistleblower protections, and penalties up to $1M. Directs the Government Operations Agency to design a “CalCompute” cloud (if funded).
Healthcare Implications: Indirect but meaningful for health AI. Safety frameworks and transparency reports strengthen due-diligence when procuring or integrating frontier models in clinical tools. Incident reporting to California's Governor's Office of Emergency Services and whistleblower protections help surface risks with public-health impact. CalCompute may expand academic/health-system access to compute for R&D and evaluation.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: H125 – Continuing Budget Operations
Summary: Session Law 2025‑89 continues prior budget authority and adjusts appropriations for the 2025–27 biennium. Sets fiscal and administrative directives across agencies and programs while a comprehensive budget is finalized.
Healthcare Implications: Although not an AI‑specific statute, continued appropriations and IT adjustments allow DHHS and partners to maintain or advance analytics modernization and pilots. Any AI procurements or health IT pilots must adhere to the allocation, oversight, and reporting structures set by the session law.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB1806 – Wellness and Oversight for Psychological Resources Act
Summary: Creates the Wellness and Oversight for Psychological Resources Act, prohibiting any person or company from using AI to provide therapy or psychotherapy or to make therapeutic decisions. Licensed professionals may still use AI for administrative or supplementary support (e.g., scheduling, billing, record management) but not for diagnosis or treatment. The Act authorizes enforcement by the Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation and includes specified exceptions (e.g., religious counseling, peer support, self‑help materials).
Healthcare Implications: Bars AI chatbots and similar tools from acting as therapists or conducting therapy in Illinois, tightening clinical safety and accountability. Behavioral health providers must ensure any AI deployed is confined to administrative support and maintain human clinical judgment for care. Vendors marketing AI therapy or decision‑making tools face enforcement risk; procurement and compliance teams should update contracts and disclosures.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HB2748 – Relating to the Use of Nursing Titles
Summary: Prohibits nonhuman entities, including AI-powered agents, from using protected nursing titles or abbreviations (e.g., APRN, RN, LPN, NP, CRNA, CNS, CMA, CNA).
Healthcare Implications: Health systems and vendors must avoid “AI nurse” branding and adjust patient-facing UX, disclosures, and marketing to reduce misrepresentation and patient confusion; supports clearer role boundaries for clinical communications.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HJR7 – Directing The Artificial Intelligence Commission To Work In Collaboration With The Secretary Of State...
Summary: Creates Delaware’s Agentic Artificial Intelligence Sandbox Program to pilot and evaluate emerging, autonomous/agentic AI systems under controlled conditions. The program empowers state officials to grant time‑limited regulatory flexibility with guardrails, accept applications from innovators, and produce public reports on outcomes, risks, and recommended statewide standards.
Healthcare Implications: Enables health‑tech startups and providers to test agentic AI tools for clinical operations or patient support (e.g., scheduling, navigation, documentation) within a supervised framework. Participants must implement privacy safeguards and risk controls, offering a pathway to gather evidence on safety, bias, and patient impact before broader deployment in hospitals or payer settings.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB96 – State Operating Appropriations for FY 2026-27
Summary: Regulates the use of artificial intelligence by health insurers by amending R.C. 3902.50 and enacting 3902.80; bill status: introduced and in Senate committee during the 136th GA. Targets AI‑driven determinations and establishes guardrails in insurance operations.
Healthcare Implications: Would limit reliance on AI in coverage determinations and utilization management, aiming to preserve due process and human review. If enacted, Ohio payers must document AI use, ensure explainability, and provide consumer remedies–affecting providers’ prior auth workflows and patient appeals.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB3936 – Restriction on AI from Covered Vendors
Summary: Prohibits installation or use of AI hardware/software/services on state IT assets if the AI is developed or owned by specified covered vendors; defines AI; updates ORS 276A.340/346/348 and aligns state cybersecurity posture.
Healthcare Implications: Affects Oregon state agencies’ procurement and deployment of AI, including health‑related systems at OHA and public hospitals interfacing with state networks. Vendors used by health programs must be vetted against ‘covered vendor’ restrictions, potentially impacting integrations, cloud services, and model hosting choices.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB149 – An Act Relating to Regulation of the Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems...
Summary: Establishes a statewide framework regulating the use of artificial intelligence systems in Texas, including consumer‑protection and transparency duties, definitions, and enforcement via civil penalties. Amends portions of the Business & Commerce Code and other statutes to address obligations of developers, deployers, and processors handling personal data where AI is involved.
Healthcare Implications: Health insurers, providers, and digital‑health vendors using AI for triage, scheduling, utilization review, or patient engagement should expect requirements for disclosures, data‑handling safeguards, and documentation that supports explanations and appeals. Compliance teams should map AI use cases to statutory duties ahead of the effective date to reduce enforcement risk.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB1188 – Electronic Health Record Requirements
Summary: Adds Chapter 183 to the Health & Safety Code to set requirements for Electronic Health Records. Requires EHRs containing patient information to be physically stored in the United States (including third‑party/cloud hosting), restricts non‑clinical data (e.g., credit score or voter registration) in EHRs, and mandates reasonable administrative/technical safeguards. Authorizes clinicians to use AI for diagnostic support with disclosure to patients and subject to medical‑board standards; requires parental access to minors’ records (with lawful exceptions). Directs regulators to ensure EHRs include fields for biological sex and sexual development disorders and that embedded decision‑support tools account for recorded biological sex. Enforcement includes injunctive relief and civil penalties; most provisions effective Sept. 1, 2025, with data‑localization storage applying Jan. 1, 2026.
Healthcare Implications: Significant operational impacts for hospitals, practices, and Electronic Health Record/cloud vendors: data localization will drive vendor due‑diligence and potential migrations; AI‑assisted diagnosis is expressly permitted but must include patient disclosure and human oversight. Mandatory biological‑sex fields affect documentation, reporting, and how CDS/AI tools leverage demographic variables; compliance programs should update templates, audit access controls, and ready for civil‑penalty exposure.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SB815 – Automated Systems in Health Benefit Claims Process
Summary: Regulates the use of certain automated systems, including algorithms that incorporate artificial intelligence, in the health benefit claims process and utilization review. Defines “artificial intelligence system” and “automated decision system” in the Insurance Code, and prohibits a utilization review agent from using an automated decision system to make, wholly or partly, an adverse determination about medical necessity or coverage.
Healthcare Implications: Directly constrains health plans’ use of AI and other automated systems in utilization review and coverage determinations. Requires that adverse determinations be made by licensed clinicians rather than by algorithms alone, which protects patients from fully automated denials, supports more transparent and clinically grounded decision-making, and enables regulatory oversight of AI tools in claims and utilization review workflows.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Equity & Bias; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: LD1727 – AI Chatbot Disclosure in Consumer Transactions
Summary: Requires businesses and other persons that use artificial intelligence chatbots in trade or commerce to clearly disclose to consumers when they are interacting with a non-human system, prohibiting the use of AI chatbots or similar technologies in a manner that misleads a reasonable consumer into believing they are communicating with a human and treating violations as unfair trade practices enforceable by the Attorney General.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to hospitals, health systems, insurers, telehealth providers, and other health-related entities that deploy chatbots for scheduling, triage, benefits, or billing in Maine, effectively requiring AI-use disclosure in patient-facing digital interactions and reinforcing transparency and consumer-protection expectations for health chatbots and other conversational AI used in care access, navigation, and customer service.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HR313 – Memorializes the United States Congress to Take Action Relative to the Use of Artificial Intelligence in Health Care
Summary: Nonbinding House Resolution urging the U.S. Congress to take action on the use of artificial intelligence in healthcare, signaling state priorities for safe and effective deployment and potential federal standards.
Healthcare Implications: No direct mandates, but indicates support for federal guardrails that could influence reimbursement, liability, and safety expectations for AI‑enabled tools. Health systems may see follow‑on state legislation echoing these themes.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: LB77 – Adopt the Ensuring Transparency in Prior Authorization Act and Provide for Insurance and Medicaid Coverage of Biomarker Testing
Summary: Comprehensive prior-authorization reform: adverse prior authorization determinations must be made by a physician (or clinical peer for non-physician requests), appeals reviewed by a same/similar-specialty physician, uniform PA forms, and firm timelines. Functionally prevents automated/AI-only denials.
Healthcare Implications: Requires human clinical accountability in utilization management decisions and speeds determinations, reshaping payer/UM vendor automation; providers gain clearer escalation paths and deemed-approved protections if timelines are missed.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: AB406 – Mental & Behavioral Health; AI Restrictions
Summary: Enacts restrictions on AI in mental and behavioral health: bars AI providers and unlicensed persons from representing AI systems as capable of providing professional mental/behavioral healthcare; prohibits offering AI that is programmed to deliver such care; and limits provider use of AI to administrative support (e.g., scheduling, billing, operational analytics). Also restricts public schools from using AI to perform the functions of school counselors/psychologists and requires an agency policy to evaluate accuracy and efficacy.
Healthcare Implications: Prevents AI chatbots or apps from being marketed or used as substitutes for licensed behavioral health providers in Nevada. Licensed providers must not use AI to deliver care directly and must independently review any AI‑generated operational outputs; violations can trigger disciplinary action and civil penalties up to $15,000 per violation. Health systems and vendors should segregate clinical‑use features from admin features and update labeling, marketing, and consent materials accordingly.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB742 – Establishes a Data Sharing and Governance Working Group within the Office of Enterprise Technology Services
Summary: Enacts a Data Sharing and Governance Working Group within the Office of Enterprise Technology Services, coordinated by the Chief Data Officer, to improve inter‑agency data sharing. The group reviews current capabilities, identifies barriers, aligns with statewide strategies, and submits reports with actionable recommendations.
Healthcare Implications: Stronger data governance and cross‑agency sharing enable safer AI use in health by clarifying data access, quality, and accountability between the Hawaii State Department of Health, Medicaid, University of Hawaii, and other partners. Expect guidance on standards for de‑identification, data linkage, and security that underpin trustworthy AI for population health and care delivery.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB820 – Health Insurance – Utilization Review - Use of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Regulates carriers’, PBMs’, and private review agents’ use of artificial intelligence, algorithms, or other software tools in utilization review. Requires determinations to account for an enrollee’s medical history and clinical circumstances; bars sole reliance on population‑level datasets; maintains clinician oversight; and sets documentation and appeal safeguards.
Healthcare Implications: Directly constrains automated prior authorization and medical‑necessity tools. Payers must implement guardrails, transparency, and human oversight; providers should expect clearer appeal documentation and be able to request human review. Aligns with national scrutiny of AI in utilization management and protects patients from algorithm‑only denials.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB2175 – Prior Authorization; Claims
Summary: Prohibits use of artificial intelligence to deny a claim or prior authorization and requires an Arizona-licensed healthcare provider to individually review any denial involving medical judgment; denial without such review is unprofessional conduct.
Healthcare Implications: Eliminates AI-only utilization management denials and forces human clinical review before adverse decisions, impacting payer workflows and any vendor rules engines; expect documentation and appeal handling changes, with oversight implications for regulators and providers.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: A3008 – Algorithmic Pricing & Artificial Intelligence Companions
Summary: Omnibus consumer-protection law that requires disclosure when prices are set using personalized algorithmic pricing and establishes safeguards for “AI companions,” including crisis-response protocols and clear disclosures that users are interacting with a bot.
Healthcare Implications: Direct implications for mental health and digital wellness tools that function as AI companions: systems must detect suicidal ideation/self-harm cues and route users to crisis services, which shapes how mental-health and wellness apps marketed in NY design, monitor, and govern AI companion features.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance; Equity & Bias
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB178 – Limit Government Use of AI Systems
Summary: Restricts state/local government uses of AI, prohibiting cognitive behavioral manipulation, discriminatory classification, and broad public‑space surveillance (with narrow exceptions). Requires disclosure when agencies use AI in public‑facing tools or publish unreviewed AI content, and mandates human review for AI outputs that affect rights or duties.
Healthcare Implications: Public‑health and human services must disclose AI use in portals/chatbots and ensure trained human review before AI‑assisted decisions affect benefits, licensing, or enforcement. AI cannot be the sole basis for determinations impacting individuals. Limits on public‑space surveillance may curb live‑video analytics in health/public‑safety contexts.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB2280 – Prior Authorization Reform Act
Summary: Overhauls prior authorization in health insurance: sets standards and timelines, requires reporting to the Insurance Commissioner, and strengthens appeals. If deadlines are missed, requests can be deemed authorized; directs studies on secure electronic submission via health records.
Healthcare Implications: Bars automated systems from issuing adverse determinations without a licensed physician; strengthens human oversight and patient protections. Payers may still use AI for intake/screening, but workflows must ensure clinical judgment, auditability, and timely responses. Electronic prior auth pushes Electronic Health Record integration and may reshape how AI prepares medical‑necessity evidence.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB956 – Consumer Protection – Workgroup on Artificial Intelligence Implementation
Summary: Establishes the Workgroup on Artificial Intelligence Implementation within the Office of the Attorney General to study AI’s consumer‑protection implications, current private‑sector uses, enforcement authority, and impacts on government‑benefits determinations, and to report recommendations to the General Assembly by July 1, 2026.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a formal venue for AI policy recommendations that can shape future health‑sector rules on transparency, bias mitigation, and consumer protections. Health systems, payers, and vendors may be called to provide input and should monitor the workgroup’s outputs for compliance impacts.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HJ4 – Interim Study of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Directs a legislative interim study of AI, including a survey of Montana, other state, and federal laws; exploration of an AI code of ethics and potential bill of rights; and analysis of transparency/disclosure requirements and civil/criminal liability for AI developers and deployers.
Healthcare Implications: Study scope includes impacts on state agencies and residents–touching health data, bias, access, and patient safety. Findings could shape future MT healthcare‑specific AI policy and procurement standards; providers, patient advocates, and vendors may wish to participate.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB1876 – Regarding the Ownership of Model Training and Content Generated by a Generative Artificial Intelligence Tool
Summary: Clarifies ownership of content generated by generative AI tools and certain model-training outputs, including work-for-hire exceptions and limitations. Establishes who owns AI-assisted outputs and recognizes contractual allocation of rights.
Healthcare Implications: For health systems, universities, and vendors building clinical/administrative AI, the law affects IP ownership of AI-assisted documentation, images, and model artifacts. Organizations should review employment and vendor contracts to align with the statute’s ownership and work-for-hire provisions. Clinical research programs using GenAI should ensure rights and disclosures are clear to avoid disputes.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems

Policy Name/Number: HB2342 – Blockchain Technology; Regulation; Computational power
Summary: Preempts cities, towns, and counties from adopting ordinances that restrict the development or deployment of artificial intelligence, computational or data center resources, or model training activities authorized by state or federal law. Establishes a statewide standard to avoid a patchwork of local AI restrictions.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare providers, payers, and vendors operating data centers or training models in Arizona benefit from uniform statewide rules rather than varying local ordinances. The law may encourage health AI R&D and hosting in-state, but it could also limit local governments’ ability to address neighborhood-level risks (e.g., noise/energy usage) from compute facilities. Compliance still requires adherence to health privacy and safety laws.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HB1958 – To Require Public Entities to Create a Policy Concerning the Authorized Use of Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Requires Arkansas public entities (state agencies, local governments, public schools, and higher-ed) to adopt an AI/automated decision tool policy that defines authorized uses, ensures human-in-the-loop review, and documents oversight. Mandates disclosures and accountability mechanisms when AI assists administrative decisions.
Healthcare Implications: Directly impacts public health agencies, public hospitals, and public universities’ health programs by requiring formal AI acceptable-use policies with human accountability. Expect new procurement and compliance checklists (e.g., documenting model purpose, data sources, and human review). Health entities should align with National Institute of Standards and Technology AI RMF and maintain audit trails for AI-assisted determinations affecting patients or benefits.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems

Policy Name/Number: SB212 – Right to Compute Act
Summary: Enacts the 'Right to Compute Act' and requires owners/operators of AI‑controlled critical infrastructure facilities to maintain a documented risk‑management policy. Defines key terms and legislative findings on computational technologies’ benefits/risks. Took effect immediately upon the Governor’s signature.
Healthcare Implications: If hospital or public‑health facilities qualify as critical infrastructure and use AI to control power, building, or logistics, they must maintain formal risk‑management policies (hazard analysis, incident response, manual fallback/override). Contracts with facilities/IT vendors may need assurances on AI controls and emergency procedures; run tabletop tests for shutdown/override drills.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HB2313 – Prohibiting the Use of the Artificial Intelligence Platform DeepSeek and other Artificial Intelligence Platforms...
Summary: Prohibits the use of the DeepSeek platform and other AI platforms controlled by a 'country of concern' on state-owned devices and networks, and restricts genetic sequencers or operational software produced by a foreign adversary.
Healthcare Implications: State labs, public hospitals, university medical centers, and health programs must audit AI tools and laboratory equipment, block prohibited platforms, and review vendor contracts and firmware/software to ensure compliance without disrupting clinical workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB178 – Nursing Practice Changes
Summary: Amends the New Mexico Nursing Practice Act to clarify the scope of practice for licensed nurses regarding anesthetics and sedation, expand Board of Nursing powers, and add a statutory definition of “artificial intelligence,” including authority for the Board to promulgate rules for the use of AI in nursing.
Healthcare Implications: Establishes explicit statewide authority and standards for how AI tools may be used in nursing practice, shaping hospital and clinic adoption of AI-enabled decision support, influencing nursing education and licensing requirements, and requiring health systems and vendors to align AI deployment with Board of Nursing rules.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SR391 – Senate Study Committee on Artificial Intelligence and Digital Currency
Summary: Creates the Senate Study Committee on Artificial Intelligence and Digital Currency to examine policy impacts, risks, and opportunities across Georgia’s economy, including public services. The committee is tasked with hearings and recommendations for the 2025–2026 biennium.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare testimony is expected on AI safety, workforce augmentation, and data privacy. Findings could shape subsequent bills on guardrails for hospital AI systems, insurer use of algorithms, and patient‑facing applications (e.g., symptom checkers), influencing compliance expectations for health entities.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB0226 – Artificial Intelligence Consumer Protection Amendments
Summary: Creates consumer‑protection requirements around AI use, including disclosures for AI‑mediated interactions and guardrails against harmful impersonation or deception. Directs enforcement via state consumer‑protection authorities and sets penalties for violations.
Healthcare Implications: Patient‑facing bots, voice agents, and automated outreach used by health systems must clearly disclose AI involvement and avoid deceptive impersonation. Marketing, scheduling, and billing workflows using generative tools should implement policy, logging, and quality assurance to meet the new standards.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HB0452 – Artificial Intelligence Amendments
Summary: Refines Utah’s AI code with additional definitions and compliance expectations for entities using AI, adding disclosure and record‑keeping duties and clarifying prohibited uses. Aligns with adjacent 2025 AI legislation to create a coherent statutory framework.
Healthcare Implications: Hospitals, payers, and vendors using AI in patient communications or eligibility/utilization management should calibrate disclosures, consent flows, and appeals processes. Documentation of AI involvement and human review points will be increasingly important for compliance and audits.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB0332 – Artificial Intelligence Revisions
Summary: Makes additional revisions to Utah’s AI statutes, consolidating definitions and adjusting agency authorities for oversight and enforcement. Coordinates with other 2025 bills to support consistent treatment of AI across sectors.
Healthcare Implications: Entities operating AI in clinical and administrative contexts should anticipate updated definitions, enforcement posture, and procurement expectations. Health programs interfacing with state systems may see new contractual assurances or audit requirements.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SB4 – An Act Relating to Protection of Information and Declaring an Emergency
Summary: Establishes a statewide, risk-based governance framework for AI used by Kentucky government; directs the Commonwealth Office of Technology to set and enforce standards; creates an AI Governance Committee; requires disclosure of public‑sector AI use and employee training; and adds synthetic‑media disclosure/cause-of-action provisions. Includes emergency provisions.
Healthcare Implications: State health agencies must adopt AI standards for eligibility, program integrity, surveillance, and other AI‑enabled systems, with inventories, privacy/security controls, human oversight, and disclosures where applicable. Procurements for AI tools will likely require compliance attestations.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SB2426 – Artificial Intelligence Regulation (AIR) Task Force
Summary: Establishes the AIR Task Force to study the use, risks, and regulatory needs of AI across sectors in Mississippi. Membership includes representatives from state agencies, universities, and technical experts. Duties include assessing AI’s impacts on healthcare, public health data management, diagnostics, and medical decision-making, and recommending safeguards and best practices.
Healthcare Implications: Directly relevant to AI in healthcare through mandated review of AI’s role in diagnostics, patient care, and public health policy, with potential to shape state health regulations and standards.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HM2 – LESC Artificial Intelligence Work Group
Summary: Creates a House memorial requesting the Legislative Education Study Committee to convene an Artificial Intelligence Work Group to study uses of AI in public education, including impacts on curriculum, student data privacy, and procurement. The work group is directed to consult stakeholders and report recommendations to interim committees.
Healthcare Implications: While education‑focused, the work group’s recommendations can influence AI governance models, procurement standards, and privacy safeguards used by public entities, including school‑based health and behavioral health programs. Cross‑sector guidance on data protection, bias, and transparency can inform New Mexico’s broader health AI policy environment and youth mental‑health screening tools used in schools.

Policy Name/Number: S07543 – Legislative Oversight of Automated Decision-Making in Government Act (LOADinG Act)
Summary: Requires New York state agencies and public authorities to inventory automated decision systems, perform algorithmic impact assessments, and publish accountability/appeal processes. Mandates reporting to the Office of Information Technology Services and periodic updates.
Healthcare Implications: State health agencies and programs (e.g., Medicaid, licensing, public health benefits) must assess risks from Automated Decision System used in eligibility, fraud detection, provider oversight, and service access. The law can drive bias audits, documentation, and avenues for contesting decisions–directly affecting patients, providers, and health IT vendors interfacing with state systems.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB896 – Generative Artificial Intelligence Accountability Act
Summary: Requires the Department of Technology (with GovOps/ODI/CalHR) to update the statewide GenAI report, directs the California Governor's Office of Emergency Services to analyze GenAI risks to critical infrastructure, and sets provenance/disclosure expectations for certain state GenAI uses. Chaptered as Ch. 928, Statutes of 2024. Implements portions of the Governor’s AI executive order in statute.
Healthcare Implications: Implications for healthcare are indirect but meaningful: state health agencies and vendors may face new reporting, provenance, and risk‑assessment expectations when GenAI touches public-facing services. Hospitals interacting with state systems should anticipate clearer disclosure and escalation paths for AI-generated communications. Risk analyses could inform preparedness planning for AI‑related threats to health infrastructure.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SB1120 – Physicians Make Decisions Act
Summary: Regulates payors’ use of AI in utilization management by requiring that medical-necessity determinations be made by qualified clinicians, not algorithms. Sets guardrails for AI-assisted review and preserves appeal rights/timelines under Knox‑Keene and Insurance Code.
Healthcare Implications: Directly limits fully automated coverage denials and compels health plans/disability insurers to maintain human clinical oversight. Providers should expect documentation standards when AI is used in utilization management and reconsider any payer contracts relying on automated denial tools. For patients, the law strengthens due process and transparency around AI-assisted determinations.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB1223 – Consumer Privacy: Sensitive Personal Information: Neural Data
Summary: Defines “neural data” (e.g., EEG/fMRI/BCI signals, eye‑tracking, neural recordings) as Sensitive Personal Information under the California Consumer Privacy Act. Extends CCPA rights and duties to neural data–right to know/limit/opt‑out, purpose limitation, and reasonable security–and prohibits selling or sharing neural data without opt‑in consent except as permitted by law.
Healthcare Implications: Neurotechnology, mental‑health apps, and medical devices that collect brain‑signal or neuro‑adjacent data must treat it as highly protected SPI, tightening consent and limiting secondary uses (e.g., AI training) outside Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act. Providers and vendors integrating BCIs or neuro‑monitoring need new data‑governance controls and contractual terms for third‑party analytics. Raises compliance expectations for de‑identification and security when neural data are combined with other patient information.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems

Policy Name/Number: AB2885 – Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Creates a uniform definition of “artificial intelligence” across multiple California codes and directs the Government Operations Agency and Department of Technology to coordinate planning around deepfakes and AI risks. Updates references to high‑risk automated decision systems inventory and certain local reporting about automation’s job impacts.
Healthcare Implications: Provides a common statutory definition agencies (including health and human services) can rely on when crafting or applying AI rules, reducing ambiguity for providers, payers, and health‑IT vendors. Supports statewide risk assessment of AI/Automated Decision System used by government programs that interact with healthcare (e.g., eligibility, fraud, benefits). Sets groundwork for future health‑specific AI guidance to align to a shared baseline.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: AB3030 – Health Care Services: Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Requires health facilities, clinics, and physician/group practices that use generative AI to generate written or verbal patient communications about clinical information to include both (1) a disclaimer indicating AI was used and (2) clear instructions for how the patient can reach a human clinician/staff. Communications reviewed by a licensed provider are exempt; violations by physicians are subject to Medical Board/Osteopathic Board jurisdiction.
Healthcare Implications: Directly impacts patient‑facing chatbots, portal messages, automated call scripts, and discharge instructions, mandating transparency and human‑escalation pathways. Drives changes to vendor contracts, quality assurance, and risk management for generative AI in care delivery. Supports patient comprehension and informed consent by clarifying when AI assists in clinical communications.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public

Policy Name/Number: AB2013 – Generative Artificial Intelligence: Training Data Transparency
Summary: Imposes training‑data documentation and transparency obligations on certain developers of generative AI models, including disclosure of data sources, copyrighted content, personal‑data handling, and known risks. Establishes posting and retention requirements and deadlines for frontier models.
Healthcare Implications: Health‑AI vendors (clinical documentation, coding, patient engagement) must provide clearer provenance for model training data, enabling health systems to evaluate privacy, consent, and bias risks before deployment. Supports safer adoption of gen‑AI in clinical and administrative workflows by improving auditability and due diligence for PHI‑adjacent datasets.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: AB1282 – Mental Health: Impacts of Social Media
Summary: Requires the Department of Public Health, in consultation with the Mental Health Services Oversight and Accountability Commission, to deliver by Dec. 31, 2026 a statewide strategy to understand, communicate, and mitigate youth mental‑health risks associated with social media. Specifies analyses, outreach, and recommendations.
Healthcare Implications: While not an AI regulation, it targets algorithmic feeds and engagement dynamics tied to adolescent mental‑health outcomes. Findings may inform clinical guidance, school‑based prevention, and evaluation metrics for digital tools and AI models assessing youth behavioral health risk.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Number: SB942 – California AI Transparency Act
Summary: Creates disclosure requirements for large generative AI providers (≥1M monthly users) to provide visible “manifest” and latent provenance for AI-generated image, audio, or video and to offer a public detection tool. Establishes enforcement via civil penalties with AG/local prosecutors.
Healthcare Implications: Healthcare communications, patient education, and marketing that use GenAI media must account for disclosure/provenance requirements when relying on covered tools. Vendors supplying GenAI components to health systems should assess whether licensing or product changes are needed to maintain required disclosures. Supports authenticity of health information and reduces risks of deepfake-related patient harm or misinformation.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Number: S3432 – Next New Jersey Program Act
Summary: Establishes the “Next New Jersey Program” within the New Jersey Economic Development Authority to spur in‑state artificial intelligence investment via up to $500M in transferable tax credits, with authority to reallocate unused Aspire/Emerge credits to AI projects. It defines eligible AI and large‑scale AI data center businesses, requires project agreements with capital investment and jobs commitments, and integrates with the 2020 New Jersey Economic Recovery Act.
Healthcare Implications: Creates a significant financing pathway for AI R&D and infrastructure that New Jersey health systems, digital health startups, and life‑science AI firms can leverage for clinical decision support, imaging, and population health applications. Hospitals/academic medical centers may partner on AI data centers and workforce initiatives funded through the program, potentially accelerating health AI commercialization and attracting talent.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems

Policy Name/Number: HB2472 – Ins-Adverse Determination
Summary: Amends Illinois utilization review requirements so that when a health plan or other utilization review program uses algorithmic automated processes in medical-necessity review, only a clinical peer may issue adverse determinations, and any such systems must rely on objective, evidence-based criteria consistent with URAC or National Committee for Quality Assurance accreditation.
Healthcare Implications: Directly regulates use of AI-enabled utilization review in health insurance, limiting fully automated adverse decisions and reinforcing human clinical oversight and evidence-based criteria for coverage decisions affecting patient access to care.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB333 – An Act to Amend Title 29 of the Delaware Code Relating to the Artificial Intelligence Commission
Summary: Establishes the Delaware Artificial Intelligence Commission to advise the Governor and General Assembly on AI risks, opportunities, and governance. The Commission surveys AI use in state agencies, engages stakeholders, and recommends standards, procurement practices, and transparency measures; initial appointments and deliverables were set in the 2024 law.
Healthcare Implications: Health and human services agencies will receive guidance on responsible AI adoption, including consent, explainability, and bias mitigation for algorithms used in benefits eligibility, utilization management, and clinical decision support. The Commission provides a forum to align health‑sector practices with statewide norms and to surface sector‑specific safeguards.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HB1688 – Use of AI by State Agencies
Summary: Creates RSA 5‑D governing AI use by state agencies: requires transparency when the public interacts with AI, mandates human review for significant decisions, and establishes guardrails on biometric identification/surveillance. Includes definitions and agency governance duties.
Healthcare Implications: Public‑sector health programs using AI (e.g., eligibility chatbots, triage, fraud analytics) must disclose AI use and add human review for impactful determinations. Restrictions on real‑time biometric ID limit live facial recognition uses in health/public safety contexts. Agencies must conduct risk assessments and implement governance when procuring AI health tools.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HR170 – Directing the Joint State Government Commission to Establish an Advisory Committee...
Summary: Implements Governor’s EO 23‑26 via the State Government Artificial Intelligence Advisory Council’s 2024 Recommended Plan and Framework, outlining principles, governance, and an action plan for trustworthy AI in state government. Emphasizes ‘return on trust’ and risk‑based deployment for essential decisions.
Healthcare Implications: Guides AI governance for state agencies, including Oregon Health Authority and partners handling Medicaid, public health analytics, and licensure systems. Promotes transparency, accountability, and human oversight practices that health programs can adopt for clinical support tools and administrative algorithms.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB24-205 – Consumer Protections for Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Creates comprehensive duties for developers and deployers of “high‑risk” AI systems making consequential decisions, including those affecting access to healthcare. Requires documented risk‑management programs, impact assessments, dataset/incident tracking, and clear consumer notices; grants the Attorney General enforcement authority. Most provisions take effect Feb 1, 2026, with phased requirements thereafter.
Healthcare Implications: Hospitals, payers, and digital health vendors using AI for diagnosis, triage, utilization review, claims, or care access are squarely within scope. Covered entities must stand up bias‑ and safety‑mitigation processes, retain detailed documentation, and provide explanations/disclosures to patients when AI informs determinations about services. Expect legal exposure for inadequate governance and a strong push to formalize model validation and monitoring in clinical workflows.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB818 – Artificial Intelligence Governance Act of 2024
Summary: Requires each unit of Maryland state government to inventory AI systems; conduct impact assessments; and, under the Department of Information Technology, adopt policies and procedures governing the development, procurement, deployment, use, and assessment of AI.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to state health entities (e.g., MDH, Medicaid) that operate AI‑enabled systems in eligibility, program integrity, and public‑health analytics. Expect standardized inventories, risk assessments, and governance processes that vendors must satisfy when contracting with the state.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB487 – JCOTS study on AI use by public bodies
Summary: Directs the Joint Commission on Technology and Science to examine and provide recommendations on the use of artificial intelligence by public bodies; authorizes study, reporting, and coordination with agencies.
Healthcare Implications: Virginia health agencies and public hospitals fall within the scope of JCOTS’ examination. Expect future standards on transparency, inventories, and human oversight for AI used in benefits, licensing, and public‑health programs.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SB7018 – Health Care Innovation Council and Revolving Loan Program
Summary: Creates the Health Care Innovation Council within the Department of Health and establishes a revolving loan program to scale promising health innovations statewide. The Council advises on priorities and oversees annual reporting.
Healthcare Implications: AI‑enabled solutions (clinical decision support, imaging triage, virtual care, revenue cycle automation) can qualify for technical assistance and financing, accelerating adoption by hospitals and community providers. Expect pilots to require outcome tracking, privacy/security compliance, and equitable access considerations to receive support.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SB5838 – Establishing an Artificial Intelligence Task Force
Summary: Creates a statewide Artificial Intelligence Task Force to study AI uses and risks across sectors; requires recommendations on governance, transparency, and workforce impacts.
Healthcare Implications: Health stakeholders are included among sectors to be consulted, with implications for standards around clinical decision support, patient communications, and equity in AI deployment in Washington.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SB0149 – Artificial Intelligence Amendments
Summary: Updates Utah’s AI statutes with definitions and governance features, including disclosures for certain AI‑generated content and standards for state use. The measure refines responsibilities for agencies and clarifies prohibited practices, with rulemaking delegated to appropriate state offices.
Healthcare Implications: State health programs and contractors deploying conversational agents or generative outputs should ensure user disclosures and human oversight. Procurements touching Medicaid, licensing, or public‑health analytics will need to align with the clarified statutory standards.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SB0150 – Artificial Intelligence and Cybersecurity
Summary: Creates the Indiana Artificial Intelligence Task Force to study and assess the use of AI by state agencies and to recommend policies on technology resources and cybersecurity. Authorizes public entities to adopt technology and cybersecurity policies, directs inventories of AI technologies in use, and clarifies state software licensing oversight.
Healthcare Implications: Indiana’s health agencies and state-run health programs must inventory and evaluate AI tools (e.g., triage chatbots, predictive analytics, fraud detection) and align with statewide cybersecurity requirements. Task Force guidance can shape procurement criteria for Electronic Health Record add‑ons, decision‑support software, and vendor risk management.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB5690 – Creating a West Virginia Task Force on Artificial Intelligence
Summary: Creates the West Virginia Task Force on Artificial Intelligence within the Office of Technology; sets membership, duties (including a report), and a termination date.
Healthcare Implications: Task force scope includes examining AI’s impacts on public services and the workforce, relevant to public hospitals and health programs. Expect recommendations that influence procurement, transparency, and risk management for AI used in state‑connected health systems.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HR3 – Creating a Select Committee on Artificial Intelligence
Summary: House Resolution creating a Select Committee on Artificial Intelligence for the remainder of the 86th Legislature to study AI issues and recommend action to the Speaker.
Healthcare Implications: Signals legislative attention to AI impacts across sectors, including healthcare delivery and patient data. Provides a venue for stakeholder input that may shape future AI guardrails affecting providers and payers.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government; Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers

Policy Name/Number: AB302 – Department of Technology: High-Risk Automated Decision Systems: Inventory
Summary: Directs the California Department of Technology to conduct and publish an inventory of high‑risk automated decision systems used, developed, or procured by state agencies; defines ADS and requires risk‑management policies and ongoing reporting.
Healthcare Implications: Covers Automated Decision System deployed by state health entities (e.g., Medi‑Cal eligibility, program integrity, triage), increasing transparency into government AI that affects access to care and benefits. Encourages bias testing and accountability measures that health providers and advocacy groups can monitor. May influence procurement standards for AI tools interfacing with public health and human services programs.
Stakeholder Tags: Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: SB01103 – An Act Concerning Artificial Intelligence, Automated Decision-Making and Personal Data Privacy
Summary: Establishes an Office of Artificial Intelligence; requires annual inventories of AI systems used by executive and judicial branches; mandates policies and procedures for state agency AI development, procurement, use, and assessments; and amends the state privacy law (including teen targeted‑ads limits).
Healthcare Implications: Covers AI governance for state health agencies (e.g., DSS, DPH), including risk assessment, transparency, and bias mitigation for automated systems that can affect benefits eligibility, licensing, or other health‑related decisions. A reference point for hospital compliance teams engaging with state systems.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public

Policy Name/Number: SCR179 – Urging Congress To Begin A Discussion Considering The Benefits And Risks Of Artificial Intelligence Technologies
Summary: Companion concurrent resolution urging Congress to weigh the benefits and risks of AI technologies and to develop policy responses. Highlights the rapid evolution of AI capabilities and the importance of oversight.
Healthcare Implications: Frames federal engagement on AI policy areas – privacy, bias, safety – that directly affect health systems’ use of AI tools. Provides context for state action and coordination with U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, U.S. Food and Drug Administration, Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology, and National Institute of Standards and Technology guidance.
Stakeholder Tags: Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB203 – Consumer Protection in Eye Care Act (Tele-Optometry Assessment Mechanisms)
Summary: Revises restrictions on the sale and dispensing of contact lenses and sets requirements for remote “assessment mechanisms,” including physician involvement, patient identity and health verification, recordkeeping, and liability coverage.
Healthcare Implications: Regulates tele-optometry and online contact lens services that rely on automated or algorithmic assessment tools, shaping how such tools can be used for vision-related care and safeguarding clinical oversight and patient safety.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: H6654 – Consumer Protection in Eye Care Act (Assessment Mechanisms)
Summary: Establishes standards and enforcement protocols for eye care services, including definitions and requirements for remote or automated “assessment mechanisms” used to generate prescriptions or recommendations.
Healthcare Implications: Constrains how telehealth and automated eye assessment technologies can be used in Rhode Island, requiring clinically supervised, standards-based use of algorithm-enabled tools for vision screening and prescriptions.
Keyword Tags: Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: H0410 – Use and Oversight of AI in State Government
Summary: Act 132 directs the development of policies and oversight for State government use of AI, including inventories, principles for trustworthy deployment, and reporting to the General Assembly. Establishes governance processes to evaluate risks and benefits of AI used by agencies.
Healthcare Implications: Vermont health agencies and partners must assess AI systems used for eligibility, licensing, and public‑health analytics under statewide policies. Vendors connecting to State systems should expect disclosure and risk‑assessment requirements.
Stakeholder Tags: Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: AB133 – Health
Summary: Health omnibus trailer bill establishing the California Health & Human Services Data Exchange Framework (DxF) with a single Data Sharing Agreement and common Policies & Procedures. Requires specified entities to sign the DSA by Jan. 31, 2023 and begin real‑time data exchange by Jan. 31, 2024 (with later timelines for some).
Healthcare Implications: The DxF expands statewide health‑information liquidity foundational to AI/analytics, care coordination, and public‑health surveillance. Providers and plans must align privacy, security, and interoperability controls with DxF requirements when using or sharing data for AI‑enabled use cases.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: SB21-169 – Protecting Consumers from Unfair Discrimination in Insurance Practices
Summary: Colorado’s first-in-the-nation insurance antidiscrimination law that restricts insurers’ use of external consumer data and information sources, algorithms, and predictive models, prohibiting unfair discrimination based on protected characteristics and requiring the Insurance Commissioner to adopt rules for specific lines of insurance that mandate testing, documentation, and governance of these models.
Healthcare Implications: Applies to health insurers’ use of external consumer data and algorithmic or AI-driven models for underwriting, pricing, marketing, and claims, pushing plans to implement governance and quantitative testing to show their models do not unfairly discriminate against protected groups; this can shape how AI-enabled risk scores, fraud analytics, and benefit design tools are built, validated, and monitored in Colorado’s health insurance market, with downstream implications for access, affordability, and equity in coverage.
Keyword Tags: Equity & Bias; Safety & Risk; Transparency & Governance; Privacy & Data
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Payers & Purchasers; Developers & Vendors; Regulators & Government

Policy Name/Number: HB2154 – Patient Access to Intelligent Personal Assistants
Summary: Requires hospitals, nursing homes, and certified nursing facilities to adopt policies that allow patient access to intelligent personal assistants (e.g., voice‑activated devices) subject to privacy and safety controls. The Board of Health must amend regulations accordingly.
Healthcare Implications: Encourages patient engagement/assistive technologies in inpatient settings, with implications for Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act/privacy safeguards, device configuration, and infection‑control protocols. Facilities should document consent and technical controls when enabling AI‑enabled devices for patients.
Stakeholder Tags: Providers & Health Systems; Patients & Public

Policy Name/Number: SJR71 – Artificial Intelligence Committee to Study and Advise Governor and Legislature on State Policy Issues
Summary: Creates a joint legislative commission to study advanced technology and AI and recommend policy options to the Legislature. The commission evaluates workforce, economic development, and regulatory impacts and reports findings back to lawmakers. Membership includes legislators and stakeholders.
Healthcare Implications: Not health-specific, but the commission’s scope includes workforce and automation issues that affect the healthcare labor pipeline and training needs. Findings could inform Alabama’s approach to artificial intelligence oversight in clinical and administrative settings. Outputs may shape future state legislation relevant to hospitals and health systems.

Policy Name/Number: H0378 – Artificial Intelligence Task Force
Summary: Creates the Vermont Artificial Intelligence Task Force to study AI impacts and recommend policies for responsible growth and State use; includes reporting deadlines and sunset.
Healthcare Implications: Although broad, the task force examined implications for public services including health, laying groundwork for later State AI governance that touches Medicaid, licensing, and health data policy.
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Developers & Vendors

Policy Name/Number: HB2300 – Mental Health Clinical Advisory Group Algorithms (ORS 414.359)
Summary: Establishes the Mental Health Clinical Advisory Group in the Oregon Health Authority to develop evidence-based algorithms for prescription drug treatment of mental health disorders in medical assistance recipients and advise on drug coverage policies.
Healthcare Implications: Creates a formal governance structure for algorithm-based mental health treatment protocols in Medicaid, influencing how algorithmic prescribing and prior-authorization tools are designed, implemented, and updated for behavioral health care.
Keyword Tags: Safety & Risk; Clinical Quality & Efficacy; Transparency & Governance
Stakeholder Tags: Patients & Public; Providers & Health Systems; Payers & Purchasers; Regulators & Government
